name: Minimize Function Arguments
source: Clean Code by Robert C. Martin, Chapter 3: Functions
problem: Functions with many arguments are hard to understand, difficult to test exhaustively, and tempt callers to pass null or wrong-order values. Flag (boolean) arguments loudly proclaim that a function does more than one thing.
steps: Aim for zero arguments (niladic), then one (monadic), then two (dyadic); treat three or more as a design warning.
When several arguments naturally belong together, extract them into a parameter object.
Replace boolean flag arguments by splitting the function into two explicitly named functions.
Avoid output arguments; instead, change state on the owning object or return a new value.
Use instance variables to share context rather than passing the same state through multiple calls.
